content.text: Re-embedding an entire document corpus every night is expensive and unnecessary. The incremental indexing pattern uses content hashing to identify which documents have changed, embedding and upserting only those, while deleting chunks for documents that have been removed. A full sync that previously cost thousands of embedding API calls shrinks to the cost of only the changed fraction.

Incremental indexing is a sync pattern for vector stores that avoids redundant re-embedding. On each sync cycle, a content hash (typically MD5 or SHA-256) is computed for every source document. The hash is compared against a persisted hash store. If the hash is unchanged, the document's chunks already in the vector store are correct — skip it. If the hash is new or different, re-chunk and re-embed the document and upsert its chunks. If a document's source hash is present in the store but the document no longer exists in the source, delete its chunks.

The pattern is called "sync-docs" in many open-source RAG frameworks because it mirrors the semantics of a file sync operation: source is truth, the vector store is a derived view, and the sync process makes the view converge to the source efficiently. The hash store is a simple lookup table — a PostgreSQL table, a Redis hash, or even a JSON file for small corpora — that maps document ID to its last-seen content hash.

Embedding API costs scale linearly with the number of tokens embedded. For a corporate knowledge base with 50,000 documents, a full nightly re-index might cost 500,000 tokens per document (after chunking and overlap), totalling 25 billion tokens — thousands of dollars per night. If typical nightly churn is 2–5% of documents, incremental indexing reduces the embedding cost by 95–98%.

Latency also matters. Full re-indexing of a large corpus may take hours. With incremental indexing, a sync cycle that processes only changed documents completes in minutes, enabling near-real-time knowledge base updates without dedicated infrastructure.
Embedding cost reduced by the fraction of unchanged documents — typically 90–99% savings on mature corpora with slow churn.
Sync latency proportional to change volume, not corpus size — enabling frequent syncs (hourly or continuous) at the cost of only the delta.
Automatic deletion of stale chunks — documents removed from the source are pruned from the vector store, preventing ghost retrieval.
Idempotent — running the sync multiple times produces the same result; safe for retry on failure.
No full-table locks or vector store downtime — upserts and deletes happen row-by-row, preserving read availability during sync.

The hash store adds a persistent dependency. If the hash store is lost or corrupted, the next sync behaves as a full re-index (every document appears changed). Hash store recovery is straightforward — repopulate it from the current source hashes — but the interim re-index is expensive. For small corpora a local JSON file is adequate; for production, use a PostgreSQL table with transactional updates.

Content hashing detects changes but cannot detect semantic drift caused by external factors. If the meaning of a document changes because it references an external glossary that was updated, the document's MD5 hash is unchanged and the sync skips it. For knowledge bases where semantic accuracy matters more than cost, a time-based re-index (weekly full sync) alongside incremental daily syncs provides a safety net.

On each sync cycle, the indexer loads the current source manifest (a list of document IDs and their current content). It computes the MD5 hash of each document's text content. It then loads the persisted hash store and computes three sets: new documents (ID not in hash store), changed documents (ID in hash store but hash differs), and deleted documents (ID in hash store but not in current source). Unchanged documents (same ID, same hash) are skipped.

For new and changed documents, the indexer re-chunks the text, calls the embedding API, and upserts the resulting vectors. The upsert uses the document ID plus chunk ordinal as the chunk ID, so re-embedding a changed document atomically replaces its old chunks. For deleted documents, the indexer deletes all chunks from the vector store where the source_doc_id matches, then removes the hash store entry.

attrs.alt: Incremental indexing with MD5 deduplication flow diagram
attrs.title: Incremental Indexing Sync-Docs Pattern

content.text: The diagram traces the sync-docs cycle: source documents are hashed, compared against the stored hashes, and routed to either skip (unchanged), embed+upsert (new/changed), or chunk deletion (orphaned). Only the changed path calls the embedding API, and the hash store is updated only after a successful upsert.

Use incremental indexing whenever the source corpus changes at a rate lower than 50% per sync cycle. At higher churn rates the overhead of computing and comparing hashes may exceed the embedding cost saved. For corpora that are append-only (new documents but never updates), simpler tracking — remember the highest ID or timestamp already indexed — avoids the hash computation altogether.

import hashlib, psycopg2, psycopg2.extras
from typing import Iterable
import voyageai

vo = voyageai.Client()

def md5(text: str) -> str:
    return hashlib.md5(text.encode()).hexdigest()

def chunk_text(text: str, size: int = 1800, overlap: int = 180) -> list[str]:
    words = text.split()
    chunks, i = [], 0
    while i < len(words):
        chunk = " ".join(words[i : i + size])
        chunks.append(chunk)
        i += size - overlap
    return chunks

def sync_docs(
    conn,
    source_docs: Iterable[dict],   # each: {id: str, text: str}
    model: str = "voyage-3-large",
) -> dict:
    source = {d["id"]: d["text"] for d in source_docs}
    source_hashes = {doc_id: md5(text) for doc_id, text in source.items()}

    # Load stored hashes
    with conn.cursor() as cur:
        cur.execute("SELECT doc_id, content_md5 FROM doc_hashes")
        stored = {row[0]: row[1] for row in cur.fetchall()}

    new_or_changed = [
        doc_id for doc_id, h in source_hashes.items()
        if stored.get(doc_id) != h
    ]
    deleted = [doc_id for doc_id in stored if doc_id not in source_hashes]

    stats = {"new_or_changed": len(new_or_changed), "deleted": len(deleted), "skipped": len(source) - len(new_or_changed)}

    # Upsert changed documents
    for doc_id in new_or_changed:
        text = source[doc_id]
        chunks = chunk_text(text)
        embeddings = vo.embed(chunks, model=model, input_type="document").embeddings
        with conn.cursor() as cur:
            # Delete old chunks first
            cur.execute("DELETE FROM doc_chunks WHERE source_doc_id = %s", (doc_id,))
            # Insert new chunks
            psycopg2.extras.execute_values(
                cur,
                "INSERT INTO doc_chunks (id, source_doc_id, chunk_text, embedding) VALUES %s",
                [(f"{doc_id}::{i}", doc_id, c, e) for i, (c, e) in enumerate(zip(chunks, embeddings))],
                template="(%s, %s, %s, %s::vector)",
            )
            cur.execute(
                """INSERT INTO doc_hashes (doc_id, content_md5, synced_at)
                   VALUES (%s, %s, now())
                   ON CONFLICT (doc_id) DO UPDATE
                   SET content_md5 = EXCLUDED.content_md5, synced_at = now()""",
                (doc_id, source_hashes[doc_id]),
            )
        conn.commit()

    # Delete orphaned documents
    for doc_id in deleted:
        with conn.cursor() as cur:
            cur.execute("DELETE FROM doc_chunks WHERE source_doc_id = %s", (doc_id,))
            cur.execute("DELETE FROM doc_hashes WHERE doc_id = %s", (doc_id,))
        conn.commit()

    return stats
Running a sync from a directory of text files

from apscheduler.schedulers.background import BackgroundScheduler
import psycopg2

conn = psycopg2.connect("postgresql://localhost/ragdb")

def run_sync():
    from your_cms import fetch_all_articles  # replace with real source
    docs = [{"id": str(a.id), "text": a.body} for a in fetch_all_articles()]
    stats = sync_docs(conn, docs)
    print(f"[sync] {stats}")

scheduler = BackgroundScheduler()
scheduler.add_job(run_sync, "interval", minutes=30)
scheduler.start()

A SaaS documentation platform indexes 120,000 help articles into a Claude RAG pipeline. Articles are updated by a team of 40 writers; average daily churn is 400–800 articles (0.3–0.7% of the corpus). Before incremental indexing, a nightly full re-index cost 8 hours and $180 in embedding fees (120,000 docs × 15,000 tokens average × $0.0001/1k tokens). After switching to hourly incremental syncs, each sync processes 25–50 changed articles in under 2 minutes at a cost of under $0.20, reducing monthly embedding spend from $5,400 to under $200 while improving freshness from 24-hour lag to under 1 hour.

Hashing the document's file metadata (mtime, inode) instead of its content is a common mistake. mtime changes when a file is touched without content changes, causing unnecessary re-embedding. Always hash the document text itself.

# WRONG: hash file metadata
import os
h = str(os.path.getmtime(filepath))  # changes on any touch

# RIGHT: hash file content
import hashlib
h = hashlib.md5(open(filepath, 'rb').read()).hexdigest()

Not deleting old chunks before upserting new ones causes ghost chunks. If a document is re-chunked with different boundaries (because its text changed), the old chunk IDs remain in the vector store alongside the new ones. The fix is to delete all chunks by source_doc_id before inserting new ones, using a transaction to keep the vector store consistent.
Use a transaction to wrap delete-old-chunks + insert-new-chunks + update-hash-store — partial sync failure leaves the store in a consistent state rather than a mix of old and new chunks.
Prefer SHA-256 over MD5 for production if documents contain user-generated content — MD5 has known collision weaknesses, though in practice collisions are extremely unlikely for document corpora.
Log the stats dict (new_or_changed, deleted, skipped) on every sync run — it surfaces corpus churn trends and unexpected spikes that indicate upstream pipeline issues.
Run a weekly full re-index alongside the incremental sync as a safety net against hash store corruption or semantic drift from external references.

Hash computation for 100,000 documents (average 10 KB each) takes under 3 seconds on a single core. The dominant cost is the database round trip to load and compare stored hashes; bulk-loading all hashes in one SELECT eliminates N+1 query overhead. For corpora above 1 million documents, shard the hash store by document ID prefix and parallelize sync workers across shards.

LangChain's RecordManager provides an implementation of this pattern that works with any LangChain vector store (Chroma, pgvector, Weaviate, Pinecone). It tracks documents by a unique source key and handles the add/update/delete lifecycle. LlamaIndex's IngestionPipeline with a docstore offers similar functionality. For custom implementations, the pattern requires only a hash store (a single PostgreSQL table or Redis hash) and a vector store with upsert support.

The hash store must be transactionally consistent with the vector store. If a crash occurs mid-sync after chunks are written but before the hash is updated, the next sync will detect the document as "changed" and re-embed it — a safe but wasteful outcome. Wrapping both writes in a single database transaction (when hash store and vector store share the same PostgreSQL instance) prevents this. When they are separate systems, implement an idempotent upsert that is safe to retry.
Hash the document content (not metadata) to detect changes — MD5 is fast enough for most corpora; SHA-256 for content with security implications.
Three sync outcomes per document: skip (unchanged), embed+upsert (new/changed), delete-chunks (orphaned).
Delete old chunks before inserting new ones within a transaction to prevent ghost retrieval from stale chunk IDs.
Typical savings are 90–99% reduction in embedding API cost on mature corpora with low daily churn.
LangChain's RecordManager and LlamaIndex's IngestionPipeline implement this pattern out-of-the-box with minimal configuration.
